text: Although I'm sharing a robotic right indirect inguinal hernia repair.
The indirect inguinal hernia is visible lateral to the epigastric vessels, appearing prominent and symptomatic.
The surgeon navigates through thin peritoneum and enters slightly above the transversalis fascia during the robotic right indirect inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon extends the surgical flap from lateral to medial on the right side, continuing the dissection process in the inguinal region.
The surgeon performs medial dissection, carefully dropping the bladder to expose the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament during the robotic right indirect inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon crosses the midline to the patient's left side, continuing the dissection in the area of the indirect inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon works laterally to re-enter the preperitoneal plane, maneuvering around the visible anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the cord structures and carefully sweeps them down from the indirect hernia sac, revealing the anatomical relationship between these structures.

As much as I like to keep these cases as bloodless as possible, you'll see in a little bit, as often as the case in these cases, you'll get a little bit of bleeding. It's not too much to make you stop what you're doing, but then all of a sudden, you realize that it doesn't look good for the video and therefore you should do something about it.

The surgeon carefully dissects the chord structures from the indirect hernia sac, pulling them down into the canal. The vas deferens is visible, cleared from the sac during this meticulous dissection.
The surgeon continues to sweep the cord structures down off the indirect hernia sac, progressing towards the tip of the sac without transecting any structures.
The surgeon observes bleeding starting laterally along the cord and in Bogros' space, with the area turning red, though it is not significant enough to halt the procedure.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for potential sources of bleeding while searching for a lipoma of the cord. No lipoma is found, and the surgeon continues to parietalize the cord structures.
Blood is visibly accumulating in the surgical field as the surgeon works, prompting the introduction of a sponge to manage the bleeding.

The surgeon uses gravity to unroll a sponge from top to bottom, exposing areas of bleeding. Short bursts of monopolar, quadrary, or bipolar energy are applied to control bleeding. Careful elevation of important structures is performed to minimize thermal spread during cauterization.
The surgeon identifies a bleeding site on the Cremaster muscle fibers located beneath the spermatic cord and lifts the cord to elevate the area for better access.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field to ensure there is no small bowel or ureter near the area of dissection. After confirming the absence of a lipoma of the cord and assessing the bleeding, the surgeon proceeds with the operation.
The surgeon places a 12x16 mesh over the hernia site, ensuring it is properly positioned to cover the defect.
The surgeon closes the flap over the surgical site, following previous efforts to manage bleeding in the area.

I've seen videos of other surgeons where they will actually leave the bleeding the way that that is. You know, it's very low volume bleeding. All of that is most likely going to stop. I just don't like the idea of leaving that blood inside the flap and touching the mesh.

I always have concerns that the patient would end up developing a hematoma, so I do think that it's important to try to establish a bloodless field and no pooling of blood near the end of the case. And that's why I try my best, number one, to prevent bleeding before it starts, and number two, to make sure that it stops before the end.
The surgeon closes the flap using a six-inch relock technique, addressing an additional rent in the flap with a 3-0 silk suture.
The surgeon places an angiocath into the flap and laparoscopically evacuates gas from the flap, visibly reducing its distension.

The surgeon presses on the scrotum to evacuate air, connecting an angiocath to suction to remove gas from the surgical site. The upper lip of the mesh is adjusted to ensure it sits flat, and suction is maintained during desufflation to keep the flap tight.
Anyway, I hope you learned and enjoyed.
